speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Mrs. Heilman. What were the sanitary conditions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sanitary conditions were awful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Obviously, there was no hot water. A cake of soap was equivalent to, to a cake of gold. And the overpopulation was tremendous. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The malnutrition, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] all of this combined together caused an epidemic of typhoid. When there was-- at the beginning, when there was a case of typhoid, the whole apartment building was blocked off and disinfected and people quarantined. But after a while, it became such an epidemic that all systems collapsed, and people were infecting one another. This was-- this is a special kind of typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not the stomach typhoid. It was the skin typhoid, very, very infectious. And people were infecting each other. Many people died, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quote, natural death. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was horrible. To maintain any kind of cleanliness was very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. My mother. She made absolutely sure that we are dressed properly, that we are cleaned properly. My father, I remember, made a shoe polish out of grease, ashes, and something black so that when we were walking out, we were-- our shoes were polished and we were dressed properly. And there were soldiers, I told you before. And we had to appear properly dressed. And we did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents were very exceptional people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about Hashomer Hatzair before. How did the resistance start?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned to you before, the seed of the resistance started at this big conference on the-- in the synagogue. There were several different youth organizations in the ghetto. I couldn't tell you what kind. But I myself was familiar with Hashomer Hatzair. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I am not aware of the policy and overall strategy. I was not part of the top hierarchy of the resistance. I was just a rookie. But I do know that the plans for the resistance of different youth organizations have taken responsibility for different graphical areas of ghetto so that each one was responsible for that particular area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Hashomer responsible for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The particular street, the area where their headquarter was, was from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH]. Further east, I believe, somewhere in [NON-ENGLISH], was different organization. I really couldn't tell you exactly. But I remember on one blockade-- it was blockade, by the way, the word that I was looking for before and couldn't remember where they were blocking the street and transporting the people out. On one blockade, I was with three or four people. Only one of them had a rifle. And we were just rookies. One of them was a messenger. We didn't have telephones. We didn't have walkie-talkies. So young boys were running from one post to another to tell them what the story is. And in this particular group, there was the commandant with our big group by the name of Shimon Heller. One of the messenger boys was running from the street and was shot. So Shimon ran after him to pick him off the street, and he was killed on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we had another runner who told us to abandon them at that post and run to another spot. What was happening is that the gang, we knew the buildings, the ruins of the building, and the alleys and we're running from post to post while the Germans were shooting down on the street. We could see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to see Jews fighting Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that it was a question at the point of Jews fighting Germans. It was us not going without resistance. You know, whoever they were-- there were Germans. There were Ukranians. There were Poles. We were not going to, to go without resistance. And this felt very good and very frightening at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for quite a while. But I wasn't there at all times. I was, as you remember, with my parents outside. I used to come in only sometimes. And at one point I was told that they cannot accept guests for security reasons. I was kicked out. I could no longer participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my parents on the Aryan side. And I used to come in to visit my sister. And at those occasions, I used to join them until one particular moment, they told me that I can no longer come, you know? Either I-- they gave me a choice. Either I am with them or not. And this was from one of the most agonizing decisions that I had to do in my life because I couldn't abandon my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was still in the ghetto. And in one of the big fights-- in the big resistance movement-- this must have-- this must have been around the Passover time. We run with one of the runners from Hashomer Hatzaie through the canals, through the burning ghetto at that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was happening at that time was that we ran into our apartment. The Germans were methodically exploding and burning all the buildings in the ghetto, one building after another. We were in our apartment on the third floor on the three-story building. Two buildings away from us was burning. The heat and the smoke from that building was so suffocating and so hot that we couldn't stay in this apartment anymore. The smock was intense. Downstairs in the yard there were Ukrainians and Germans with rifles. They were shooting. And they were screaming, people out, people out. We jumped from the third floor windows into the yard, into the [INAUDIBLE]. But the smoke were so intense, and people were jumping from all over the place, that they were shooting indiscriminately. And we succeeded to run. We had scraped knees and elbows and nothing else. And we ran through the aisle, through the ruins of the burning building with this one runner through the canals. And when we were running through the canals, we could feel the gas being pumped into the canals behind us. But we succeeded to get out on the Aryan side. And we ran to join our parents. And we stayed there until May 3, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was finished, quote. And we were told by our captain that the Germans will come and take us. He gave us a way out. We were forewarned. My father was trying to make some arrangements to have us hidden on the Aryan side under non-Jewish papers. But before we could get organized, the SS came and transported us to the Umschlagplatz. We were coming, walking through the still burning ruins of the ghetto to the Umschlagplatz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a horrible stench, a very peculiar kind of a stench of burning flesh, rubber. What used to be a ghetto was totally ruins. There were mountains of stone, smoke. And you could see people jumping from one pile to another and disappearing. It was a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] indescribable scene. When we came to Umschlagplatz, there was a sea of humanity. People were sitting on their parcels holding them. And there were-- the Umschlagplatz was also in one of those buildings that used to be, before the war, a public school. They were all built in the same kind of a system.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how long we were there. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] One day? I don't know. It was overnight, two nights, two days, what. From there we were taken to the waiting train. How many people were there around?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Umschlagplatz?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. 1000? More? I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were taken out to the cattle train. And we were packed. I know that in our wagon, there were 150 people because they were counting how many. And 150, and they closed the doors. If you think of a cattle wagon, I don't know the exact dimension. But this is about 3/4 [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] of a city bus at the most, like a city bus. And if you can imagine 150 people, each one dragging a possession that looks like a knapsack with whatever they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with you parents and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was my parents, and my sister, and I. At the beginning, there were some people that were trying to avoid panic and asked people to sit if at all possible so that-- there were two small little windows-- to let the air come in. But it didn't take long that people were in a panic. And the younger-- the younger and the stronger monopolized the little windows. People were trampling on each other. And I remember my sister and I were bodily keeping people from trampling my mother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how long this trip took. But when we came to a forking of the road, again there was some expert that were telling us that we are not going to Treblinka, which was well known by that time. It was a death camp. We're going to Auschwitz. And this was the better of the two. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There was no food. There were no toilet facilities. And there was no water. And this was worst of all. The thirst was terrible. Anyway, our train came to Majdanek. And as we came out, half of the people were dead and half of the people were mad. It was raining when we arrived to Majdanek. And the Germans with rifles and dogs barking were waiting for us. And again, there was screaming and yelling. Out! Out! Out! We had to jump. It was quite high from the wagons to the ground. And I remember I leaned down to scoop up a little bit of mud to quench my thirst. And I got a whip on my back. And the screaming was run, run, run. And we were running. We were running through an obstacle course because you couldn't run with all the belongings weighing on you. I threw out my rucksack. And everybody before and after me were throwing whatever they had. So all this possession was thrown on the ground. My mother kept her rucksack with her. And we came to a spot where a young SS man called me. I left my mother, and I went over to him. And he pointed with a finger to go in to the right. And I went to pick my mother up. And he said, no, no. And my mother went straight. And this was the last time I saw her. I was taken to a sauna, which is a German for a washroom. I forgot to tell you that the men and women were separated as we came from the train. So I lost my father right then and there. And in the sauna, I have found my sister, whom I have lost on the way. So the two of us were there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the last time both of us saw our parents. This was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in Majdanek. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were told to take off all our clothes, but we could take our shoes. Went through cold water showers and we were given some clothes to wear that. And we're taken into the camp. Those were wooden barracks, brand new. They smelled wonderful with fresh wood. And there was nothing else there. We slept on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day we used to go out on appels, which are the role calls. And we're working in the field. And I remember we were asking-- they were all Czech prisoners. We were asking them where our parents were. And they were very kind. They used to tell us that we are young and strong. And we work hard work, but our parents have easier work somewhere in other camps. And we believed them. So we volunteered to work out in the fields by any chance maybe to see them. Well, we never did. In Majdanek when we're working in the fields, they had horseradish growing. So we used to eat the horseradish, which was, you know, something good to eat. We didn't stay in Majdanek very long. It was-- we arrived there in May. We left, I believe, in September. We arrived to Birkenau. I believe it was September. I'm not quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. But it was nothing like the transport from Warsaw to Majdanek. Don't even remember the details. It was nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Birkenau, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] the first thing that was done, our hair was shorn. Our numbers were tattooed on our left arm. We went through the showers. And then we were given a Russian prisoner of war's uniforms to wear. Obviously, there were Russian prisoners there before us. And this is what we were given to wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to have your number tattooed on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that at that point we were so numb that we reacted more to our hair being shorn than to the number. We couldn't recognize each other without our hair. The numbers were tattooed by other prisoners. Some of them knew how to write, and some didn't. And lucky for me, the one that tattooed my number knew how to write so that my number is quite legible. She was also a Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the number. You see, this is 48,150. The triangle denotes Jews. The non-Jews did not have a triangle. They had nothing. This number's when-- by the number, you can tell which transport it is from, where it came, because it went in numerical order. So you know exactly which transports you came from. It was until 1944. In 1944, they started numbers again. And they started was an A at the beginning. And they put it on the inside of the arm rather than on the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: The girl that tattooed my number looked at it and told me, you are going to come out of here alive. I said, how can you tell that? And he said, because the total of your numbers is 18. And 18 in Hebrew spells chai, which means life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] From there, we were taken to camp B to a quarant-- quarantine, quarant-- Quarantine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarantine. It took about two weeks, a month. I don't know exactly. It was a horrible scene. Quarantine means that you didn't work. You stayed there. In the morning they had to-- we used to get up at about-- who knows-- 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock in the morning. It was pitch dark. And we're staying-- standing for hours on this famous roll call. It was bitter cold. They were cleaning the block. So we couldn't go back in. So we were driven to what was called a Wiese in German, which is a meadow. It was just a square of sand. And when we came, there were Greek girls before us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were walking corpses. We had never seen anything like it. They used to sit on the ground totally without any strength, without any energy. Their eyes were dead. Their-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] their bones were looking through their skin. And their occupation was to look for lice in each other. And this was going on for hours. We were looking, keeping, and sitting. Alive corpses. It was horrible. There was a name for them in Auschwitz. Thy used to call them Muselmanns. I don't know why. This is-- was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barracks, were there prisoners who had been there longer, who told you what was going on or what to expect? How to--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember that, but I remember those that were-- had official duties, who were sadistic, really, and very mean, who used to tell us, you look good now. Just wait. This is not a hotel. This is not a guest house. And you are to listen to us. And we knew that there were selections. And we knew that there were lice. And we knew that in selection, if you have a little scratch, you will be sent to gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came into the barracks, the barracks-- I don't know-- they were made out of bricks. And they had three stories opening, one on the floor, one in the middle, and one on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight of us were on each level. So there were 24 in one little space. There were about, I would say, eight, 16, 72 on each side, 64 on both sides. 64 times eight-- no. There was more. There was more. Anyway, the first thing we did when we came to the barracks was throw out the mattresses because they were full of lice. And we were sleeping on the wooden boards so that not to have lice, eight of us. And at night, you just didn't know what to do with your legs. And somebody else's legs were in your face. And there were-- and that your legs were under you. It was terrible. People were stealing the blankets. Before you came you had to steal somebody else's if you wanted to have one. And our most important possession was a red enamel bowl. Without this bowl and without a spoon, you wouldn't get your supper. You just wouldn't. So we used to try them. They had a little hole. We used to tie them up to our bodies to keep them so that they won't get stolen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much food did you receive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We received-- it's an interesting story. We were supposed to receive a fourth of very hard, small loaf of bread. But those that were in charge sometimes used to cut it into five. They used to cut a slice in the middle and then put it back. So actually we got only a fifth instead of a fourth of a bread. Well, this was a reality of life.
